prevalence.source: VIGITEL (Vigilância de Fatores de Risco e Proteção para Doenças Crônicas por Inquérito Telefônico)
prevalence.trend: increasing due to rising obesity and dietary changes

riskFactors: High H. pylori infection rates (50-70% prevalence in Brazil)
Dietary habits including frequent consumption of spicy foods, coffee, and fatty meals
Obesity and overweight (prevalent in urban populations)
vulnerablePopulations: Low-income urban dwellers with limited healthcare access
Indigenous and rural communities with high H. pylori exposure
Obese adults in southern and southeastern regions
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Based on Rome IV criteria adapted by SBH (Sociedade Brasileira de Hepatologia), including upper endoscopy for confirmation, symptom assessment (heartburn, epigastric pain), and H. pylori testing via urea breath test or serology

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: Low routine screening (under 20% coverage via SUS for symptomatic cases)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: 55% managed with lifestyle and medication adherence

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: SUS protocol emphasizes clinical history, endoscopy when available, and H. pylori eradication testing
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: PPIs (e.g., omeprazole) and H2 blockers provided free via SUS; H. pylori treatment with triple therapy (amoxicillin, clarithromycin, PPI) for confirmed cases
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Limited access to endoscopy in rural and northern regions; high H. pylori prevalence (up to 70% in Northeast Brazil) due to sanitation issues
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: SUS ensures free access to essential PPIs and antibiotics, reducing out-of-pocket costs for low-income patients
